rule: When a linkDown(2) trap is generated to indicate a communication link failure, the sending SNMPv1 agent must include as the first variable-binding pair in the Trap-PDU the ifIndex object instance identifier and its corresponding value that identifies the specific interface where the failure occurred. This requirement applies during trap generation when the protocol entity detects a link failure condition, ensuring that management systems receiving the trap can immediately determine which interface experienced the outage. The ifIndex instance must be valid and correspond to an interface currently configured in the agent's management information base.

When a linkUp(3) trap is generated to indicate that a communication link has transitioned to an operational state, the sending SNMP entity must construct the Trap-PDU such that the first variable-binding pair in the variable-bindings field contains the ifIndex object identifier and its corresponding instance value identifying the specific interface that has come up. This requirement applies during trap generation when the protocol entity detects a link-up event on any configured interface, ensuring that management systems can immediately identify the affected interface from the trap payload without requiring additional queries.
req_fields: instance value identifying the specific interface

rule: When constructing SNMP PDUs, certain operations (such as GetRequest-PDU) require only the variable name in the VarBind, not its associated value. In such cases, the protocol entity must ignore the value field during processing, but the value portion must still contain syntactically valid ASN.1 encoding. It is recommended to use ASN.1 NULL as the placeholder value to ensure proper protocol compliance while maintaining efficient bandwidth usage. This applies specifically to request PDUs where the purpose is to query rather than modify managed objects, and the receiving entity must validate the ASN.1 structure while disregarding the actual content of the value field.

The GetResponse-PDU is generated by a protocol entity exclusively in response to receiving a valid GetRequest-PDU, GetNextRequest-PDU, or SetRequest-PDU, as detailed in the relevant sections of this specification. This rule governs the request-response mechanism of SNMPv1, ensuring that responses are only triggered by corresponding management operations. The protocol entity must first validate the incoming request PDU and then construct a GetResponse-PDU containing the appropriate request-id, error-status, error-index, and variable-bindings before transmission. The receiving entity subsequently processes the response and presents its contents to the SNMP application entity for further handling.

A linkUp(3) trap MUST be generated by the sending protocol entity when it detects that one of the communication links configured in the agent's management information base has transitioned to an operational state. This trap applies during the SNMP agent's runtime monitoring phase when interface state changes occur. Upon triggering, the agent SHALL construct a Trap-PDU with type linkUp(3) where the first variable-binding pair MUST contain the ifIndex object instance identifying the specific interface that became active. The expected outcome is the notification delivery to configured management stations, enabling network management systems to track interface availability and update topology status accordingly. Prerequisites include proper interface configuration in the agent's MIB and successful initialization of SNMP trap mechanism capabilities.
req_fields: first variable-binding pair containing ifIndex object instance

rule: The GetResponse-PDU maintains identical structure and field definitions to the GetRequest-PDU, differing only in the PDU type identifier. This rule applies when a protocol entity generates a response to received GetRequest, GetNextRequest, or SetRequest PDUs. The expected outcome is that the response preserves all original request parameters (request-id, error-status, error-index, and variable-bindings) while clearly indicating the response type. Prerequisites include successful receipt and processing of a valid request PDU, with the response generation triggered only by these specific request types as defined in the protocol specification.

A variable binding, or VarBind, refers to the pairing of the name of a managed object instance (ObjectName) with its corresponding value (ObjectSyntax), forming the fundamental data unit for SNMPv1 protocol operations. This construct applies when encoding or decoding protocol data units (PDUs) for all SNMP operations, including GetRequest, GetNextRequest, SetRequest, GetResponse, and Trap. The expected outcome is a properly structured SEQUENCE containing both the object identifier and its associated value, enabling the protocol to either retrieve or modify management information. Prerequisites include valid ASN.1 encoding of both components, though for request PDUs that only require object names (like GetRequest), the value field must still contain syntactically valid ASN.1 (typically NULL) even though it is ignored during processing.

This rule governs the construction of OBJECT IDENTIFIER names for address translation equivalence entries in the SNMP atTable (Address Translation Table, analogous to an ARP cache). It applies when forming instance identifiers for querying or referencing specific address translation mappings through SNMP operations. The expected outcome is a properly formatted OBJECT IDENTIFIER of the form s.w, where s represents the interface index (atIndex value) and w represents the network address OBJECT IDENTIFIER (formatted as 1.a.b.c.d for IP addresses). Prerequisites include: the address translation equivalence e must exist in the table, both atIndex and atNetAddress object instances must be properly defined for that equivalence, and the network address must follow the specified naming convention (1.a.b.c.d). For example, to reference the physical address mapping for IP address 89.1.1.42 on interface 3, the identifier would be constructed as atPhysAddress.3.1.89.1.1.42.

The GetResponse-PDU is generated by an SNMP agent in response to received GetRequest-PDU, GetNextRequest-PDU, or SetRequest-PDU messages. This PDU uses ASN.1 encoding with type tag [2] and contains four mandatory fields: request-id (to correlate with the original request), error-status (indicating success or failure), error-index (identifying which variable caused an error), and variable-bindings (containing the requested management information). Upon generation, the agent transmits this PDU back to the manager, where the receiving protocol entity extracts and presents the response data to the SNMP application layer for processing. The PDU structure is identical to request PDUs except for the type identifier, ensuring consistent parsing while distinguishing response messages.

This rule governs the naming convention for object instances in the ipAddrTable of SNMPv1, specifically for object types derived from the ipAddrEntry prefix. It applies when an SNMP manager requests or references managed objects within the ipAddrTable during protocol operations such as GET, GETNEXT, or SET. The expected outcome is that each instance of such an object type is uniquely identified by an OBJECT IDENTIFIER formed by appending the IP address (in dotted notation) of the relevant network element to the object type's name. Prerequisites include: the object type must be defined under the ipAddrEntry prefix in the MIB, and the IP address used must correspond to an existing entry in the ipAddrTable, as defined by the ipAdEntAddr object. For example, the instance for the network mask of IP address 89.1.1.42 is named ipAdEntNetMask.89.1.1.42.

Variable bindings are fundamental constructs used in SNMPv1 protocol data units (PDUs) to exchange management information between entities. A VarBind is defined as a SEQUENCE containing two components: name (ObjectName) identifying a managed object instance, and value (ObjectSyntax) representing its current or requested value. A VarBindList is defined as a SEQUENCE OF VarBind, forming an ordered list of variable bindings. This construct applies when encoding and decoding all PDU types that carry management data, including GetRequest, GetNextRequest, GetResponse, and SetRequest PDUs. The expected outcome is proper pairing of managed object identifiers with their corresponding values, enabling protocol entities to process requests and responses accurately. For PDUs that only require object names (such as GetRequest), the value field must still contain valid ASN.1 syntax (recommended to be NULL), though the content is ignored during protocol processing.

When an enterpriseSpecific(6) trap is generated, the sending protocol entity must populate the specific-trap field with a value that uniquely identifies the particular enterprise-specific event that triggered the trap. This field serves as the primary identifier for the specific trap condition within the context of the enterprise-defined trap types, enabling receiving management entities to distinguish between different enterprise-specific events. The rule applies during trap generation when the generic-trap field is set to enterpriseSpecific(6), and the expected outcome is that the receiving entity can correctly interpret and process the specific trap based on this identifier.

When a sending SNMPv1 protocol entity generates a Trap-PDU with the generic-trap field set to enterpriseSpecific(6), this indicates that the entity has detected an enterprise-specific event defined outside the standard MIB-II trap types. This rule applies during trap generation when the event falls under vendor or implementation-specific categories rather than predefined generic traps. The specific-trap field must then be populated with an enterprise-defined value to identify the particular event subtype. The receiving management station should interpret this trap as a custom event requiring enterprise-specific MIB definitions for proper processing.

This rule governs the construction of OBJECT IDENTIFIER names for identifying specific instances in the TCP connection table (tcpConnTable) within SNMP v1 management operations. The rule applies when network management systems need to uniquely reference individual TCP connections for monitoring or configuration purposes, particularly during SNMP Get, Set, or GetNext operations targeting TCP connection-related objects.

The expected outcome is a structured OBJECT IDENTIFIER that uniquely identifies a TCP connection instance by combining its local and remote addressing information in the format: local-address.local-port.remote-address.remote-port, where each IP address component is represented in dotted-decimal notation across four octets.

Prerequisites for applying this rule include:
1. The SNMP agent must implement the TCP-MIB with the tcpConnTable
2. Valid TCP connection entries must exist in the connection table
3. All referenced object instances (tcpConnLocalAddress, tcpConnLocalPort, tcpConnRemoteAddress, tcpConnRemotePort) must be accessible and contain valid values

For example, to identify the state of a TCP connection between local address 89.1.1.42 port 21 and remote address 10.0.0.51 port 2059, the resulting OBJECT IDENTIFIER would be constructed as: tcpConnState.89.1.1.42.21.10.0.0.51.2059
req_type: SNMP Get, Set, or GetNext operations

rule: The generic-trap field in the Trap-PDU must be interpreted according to standardized values defined in the SNMPv1 protocol specification. This rule applies when a receiving protocol entity processes an incoming Trap-PDU and needs to determine the nature of the reported event. The generic-trap field must contain one of the predefined values: coldStart (0), warmStart (1), linkDown (2), linkUp (3), authenticationFailure (4), egpNeighborLoss (5), or enterpriseSpecific (6). When the value is enterpriseSpecific, the specific-trap field provides additional enterprise-defined trap information. This interpretation occurs after the Trap-PDU is successfully received and before its contents are presented to the SNMP application entity. The receiving entity must correctly map these values to their corresponding event types to ensure proper trap handling and processing.

An authenticationFailure(4) trap is generated when the sending SNMP entity receives a protocol message that fails authentication validation. This rule applies during message processing when an incoming SNMP request contains invalid authentication credentials or community strings. The expected outcome is that the receiving entity generates and transmits this trap to notify the network management system of the authentication failure, while simultaneously discarding the unauthenticated message without further processing. Implementations must support trap generation capability but may provide mechanisms to suppress trap emission based on local configuration. Prerequisites include: the SNMP entity must be configured as a trap originator, and the received message must pass initial protocol syntax checks but fail authentication verification.

When an SNMPv1 agent generates an egpNeighborLoss trap to indicate that an EGP neighbor relationship has been terminated and the peer connection is no longer valid, the Trap-PDU must include the network address of the affected neighbor as the first variable-binding pair. Specifically, the initial element in the variable-bindings field shall consist of the object identifier for egpNeighAddr along with its corresponding IP address value, enabling management systems to immediately identify which specific EGP neighbor has been lost. This rule applies during trap generation following EGP neighbor state transition to down, and requires that the agent has maintained valid EGP neighbor information prior to the connection loss.

The GetNextRequest-PDU shares identical structure and field definitions with the GetRequest-PDU, differing only in the PDU type identifier. This rule applies when an SNMP application entity requests retrieval of the lexicographically next available object in the MIB view. The receiving protocol entity must process the request by first validating that each requested object name lexicographically precedes an available object, then returning the immediate successor object's name and value for each valid binding. If validation fails due to non-existent objects, size limitations, or other errors, the entity must return an appropriate error response with matching request-id. The expected successful outcome is a GetResponse-PDU containing the lexicographical successors of all requested objects with error-status set to noError.

When processing a GetNextRequest-PDU, if the receiving SNMP entity determines that the size of the GetResponse-PDU containing the lexicographical successor values for all requested objects would exceed a local implementation limitation (such as maximum PDU size), the entity must immediately terminate normal processing and return an error response. The response must be a GetResponse-PDU that maintains the same request-id as the original request but with the error-status field set to "tooBig" and the error-index field set to zero, indicating that the response size limitation applies to the entire request rather than a specific variable binding. This rule takes precedence over normal successful response generation but applies only after verifying that no previous error conditions (such as invalid object names) have been encountered.

The Trap-PDU is generated by an SNMP agent to asynchronously notify management stations of significant events, and its structure must follow a specific format. This rule applies when an SNMP application entity requests the generation of a trap notification, typically in response to network events or system state changes. The Trap-PDU must consist of an IMPLICIT SEQUENCE containing: enterprise (identifying the trap source using sysObjectID), agent-addr (source address), generic-trap (predefined event types: coldStart, warmStart, linkDown, linkUp, authenticationFailure, egpNeighborLoss, or enterpriseSpecific), specific-trap (additional event code, always present regardless of generic-trap value), time-stamp (time since last system reinitialization), and variable-bindings (event-specific data). The expected outcome is proper delivery of structured event information to receiving SNMP entities, which then present the trap contents to their application entities for processing. Prerequisites include: the SNMP application entity must initiate trap generation, and the agent must have valid values for all required fields, though destination address selection and variable-bindings interpretation remain implementation-specific.

When processing a GetNextRequest-PDU, if none of the preceding error conditions (noSuchName, tooBig, or genErr) are triggered, the receiving protocol entity must construct and send a GetResponse-PDU where each variable-binding entry contains the lexicographically next available object name and its corresponding value from the relevant MIB view. This rule applies only after verifying that: (1) all requested object names lexicographically precede available objects in the MIB view, (2) the response size does not exceed local limitations, and (3) no retrieval errors occur. The successful response must set error-status to noError, error-index to zero, and preserve the request-id from the original GetNextRequest-PDU, effectively providing the immediate successors for table traversal and hierarchical data navigation operations.

This rule governs the error handling for GetNextRequest operations when a lexicographical successor object exists but cannot be retrieved due to unspecified errors. It applies during the processing of a GetNextRequest-PDU after verifying that the requested object names lexicographically precede available MIB objects (rule 1) and that response size limitations are not exceeded (rule 2). When the receiving SNMP entity successfully identifies the lexicographical successor for an object in the variable-bindings field but encounters retrieval failures not covered by previous rules—such as internal system errors, access violations, or implementation-specific issues—this rule triggers. The expected outcome is that the entity sends a GetResponse-PDU with the error-status field set to genErr (general error) and the error-index field indicating the position of the problematic object in the original request. This ensures that the requestor receives specific error information while maintaining protocol consistency, allowing for proper error diagnosis without disrupting the entire request processing flow.

This rule governs the behavior of an SNMP agent when processing a GetNextRequest-PDU, specifically addressing the case where a requested object name does not have a valid lexicographical successor in the accessible MIB view. The rule applies immediately upon receipt of a GetNextRequest-PDU, after the agent has parsed the variable-bindings field and before attempting to retrieve any actual values. 

When this condition is met - meaning that for any object name in the variable-bindings, there is no lexicographically subsequent object available for GET operations within the agent's MIB view - the agent must respond with a GetResponse-PDU containing an error-status of "noSuchName". The response must maintain the same structure as the request, with the error-index field indicating the specific position (starting from 1) of the problematic object name in the original variable-bindings list.

This error condition typically occurs when the requested object name represents either the last object in the MIB tree or an object beyond what exists in the agent's implementation, or when the requesting entity lacks sufficient access rights to view subsequent objects. The rule ensures that SNMP managers can reliably detect when they have reached the end of available MIB objects during table traversal operations.

When generating a GetResponse-PDU in response to a GetNextRequest-PDU, the receiving SNMP protocol entity must copy the request-id value from the received GetNextRequest-PDU into the request-id field of the corresponding GetResponse-PDU. This applies to all GetResponse-PDU scenarios, including successful responses (where error-status is noError), error responses (such as noSuchName, tooBig, or genErr), and partial responses. This request-id matching ensures proper request-response correlation between the SNMP manager and agent, allowing the originating application to associate each response with its corresponding request regardless of the processing outcome.

When a receiving SNMP protocol entity successfully processes a GetRequest-PDU without encountering any errors specified in rules (1) through (4) - meaning all requested objects exist in the MIB view, are not aggregate types, fit within size limitations, and can be successfully retrieved - the entity must generate a GetResponse-PDU with the error-status field set to noError and the error-index field set to zero. This indicates successful completion of the get operation, and the response must also include the actual values of all requested objects in the variable-bindings field while maintaining the same request-id as the original request.

This rule governs the validation of ASN.1 encoding in SetRequest-PDU operations. When a receiving SNMP entity processes a SetRequest-PDU, it must verify that each value in the variable-bindings field conforms to the ASN.1 type, length, and value requirements defined for the corresponding MIB object. If any value fails this validation, the receiving entity must immediately terminate processing and send a GetResponse-PDU with error-status set to badValue and error-index indicating the position of the invalid variable-binding in the original request. This validation occurs after checking object accessibility but before attempting any variable assignments, ensuring that only properly formatted values are processed for modification.

The SetRequest-PDU shares identical structural formatting with the GetRequest-PDU, differing only in the PDU type identifier (SetRequest uses type [3] while GetRequest uses type [0]). This structural consistency applies when a protocol entity generates a SetRequest-PDU at the request of its SNMP application entity to modify MIB object values. Upon receipt, the receiving entity must validate the request against the following conditions: object availability for set operations, value type/length consistency with variable requirements, response message size limitations, and general assignment feasibility. If all validations pass, the receiving entity performs the requested assignments as an atomic operation and returns a GetResponse-PDU with error-status "noError". If any validation fails, it returns a GetResponse-PDU indicating the specific error condition while maintaining the identical response structure format.

If none of the preceding error conditions (noSuchName, badValue, tooBig, or genErr) are triggered during the processing of a SetRequest-PDU, then for each object specified in the variable-bindings field of the received message, the corresponding value is assigned to the managed object. This assignment must be performed as an atomic operation where all variable assignments in the same SetRequest-PDU are applied simultaneously. Following successful assignment, the receiving entity must send a GetResponse-PDU with error-status set to noError and error-index set to zero to indicate successful completion of the set operation.

This rule applies during the processing of a SetRequest-PDU in SNMPv1 when the receiving entity determines that the size of the generated GetResponse-PDU would exceed local implementation constraints. The rule triggers after the receiving entity has validated the request against other error conditions (such as object availability and value consistency) but before attempting to perform the actual variable assignments. When this condition is met, the receiving entity must construct a GetResponse-PDU that mirrors the original SetRequest-PDU in structure, but with the error-status field set to "tooBig" and the error-index field set to zero, indicating that the response message would be too large to transmit. This ensures the protocol can handle size limitations gracefully while maintaining the request-response correlation through identical request-id values.

This rule applies during the processing of a SetRequest-PDU when the receiving SNMP entity encounters an object in the variable-bindings field that cannot be modified due to reasons not addressed by previous error conditions (such as noSuchName, badValue, or tooBig). This serves as a catch-all error case for set operation failures. When this condition occurs, the receiving entity must generate a GetResponse-PDU that mirrors the original request in structure, but with the error-status field set to "genErr" (general error) and the error-index field indicating the position of the problematic object within the variable-bindings list. This ensures the requesting entity receives specific feedback about which object caused the failure when no other predefined error condition applies.

This rule governs the SNMPv1 protocol behavior when a SetRequest-PDU is received and one or more requested objects cannot be modified due to access restrictions. The rule applies during the processing phase of an incoming SetRequest-PDU, after the message has been received and parsed but before any variable assignments are attempted. 

Prerequisites: The receiving entity must have successfully parsed the SetRequest-PDU and identified the variable-bindings field containing the objects to be modified. The entity must also have access to the relevant MIB view definitions to determine set operation availability.

Expected Outcome: When this rule is triggered because an object in the variable-bindings is not available for set operations in the current MIB view, the receiving entity must immediately cease processing the request and return a GetResponse-PDU with the error-status field set to "noSuchName" and the error-index field indicating the position (starting from 1) of the problematic object in the variable-bindings list. The response should maintain the same request-id and structure as the original SetRequest-PDU, ensuring the originator can correlate the error with the specific failed object.

The SetRequest-PDU is generated by an SNMP protocol entity at the request of its application entity to modify managed object values. This PDU uses ASN.1 encoding with PDU type [3] and follows the structure: SetRequest-PDU ::= [3] IMPLICIT SEQUENCE { request-id RequestID, error-status -- always 0 ErrorStatus, error-index -- always 0 ErrorIndex, variable-bindings VarBindList }. 

The rule applies when an SNMP manager initiates a write operation to modify MIB object values. Upon receipt, the agent must validate the request against the following conditions: object availability for set operations in the relevant MIB view, correct ASN.1 type/length/value encoding, response message size limitations, and absence of other assignment errors. 

If all validations pass, the agent assigns values to all specified variables simultaneously and returns a GetResponse-PDU with error-status "noError". If any validation fails, the agent returns a GetResponse-PDU with appropriate error-status (noSuchName, badValue, tooBig, or genErr) and error-index indicating the problematic variable binding. The request-id must be preserved in all responses to correlate requests with replies.

When a receiving SNMP entity successfully processes a SetRequest-PDU and all variable assignments are completed without encountering any errors (meaning none of the previous error conditions in sections 4.1.5(1) through 4.1.5(4) apply), the entity must send a GetResponse-PDU back to the message originator. This response PDU must maintain the same structure and request-id as the original SetRequest-PDU, but with the error-status field explicitly set to "noError" and the error-index field set to zero, indicating successful completion of all set operations specified in the variable-bindings field.

When processing a GetRequest-PDU, if the receiving SNMP entity determines that the size of the GetResponse-PDU containing the requested variable values would exceed a local implementation-defined size limitation (such as maximum packet size), the entity must send a GetResponse-PDU with the error-status field set to "tooBig" and error-index field set to zero. This rule applies after successful validation of object names and types but before attempting to retrieve actual variable values. The response maintains the original request-id but indicates that the response cannot be generated due to size constraints, allowing the originator to potentially retry with a smaller request.

The GetRequest-PDU structure must consist of a sequence containing request-id, error-status (which must always be set to 0), error-index (which must always be set to 0), and variable-bindings. This rule applies when an SNMP application entity requests a protocol entity to generate a GetRequest-PDU for retrieving management information. The receiving protocol entity processes this PDU according to specific error-handling procedures: if all requested objects are valid and retrievable within the MIB view, it returns a GetResponse-PDU with the requested values and error-status set to noError; otherwise, it returns appropriate error codes (noSuchName, tooBig, or genErr) with corresponding error-index values. The request-id must be preserved in the response to match requests with their corresponding replies.

When processing a GetRequest-PDU, if any object specified in the variable-bindings field is an aggregate type (as defined in the SMI, such as tables or other structured objects that cannot be directly retrieved as single values), the receiving entity must immediately terminate processing and return a GetResponse-PDU. This response must mirror the original request in form, but with the error-status field set to "noSuchName" and the error-index field indicating the position (starting from 1) of the problematic aggregate object within the variable-bindings list. This rule applies during the initial validation phase before any value retrieval attempts and ensures that requests for unsupported object types are properly rejected with precise error identification.

This rule governs the SNMPv1 GetRequest-PDU error handling behavior when the requested object identifier (OID) is not accessible. The rule applies immediately upon receipt of a GetRequest-PDU during the protocol message processing flow. 

Prerequisites: The receiving SNMP entity must have successfully parsed the GetRequest-PDU and identified the variable-bindings field containing the requested object names. The entity must also have access to the relevant MIB view defining which objects are available for get operations.

Expected Outcome: If any object name in the variable-bindings field does not exactly match an object available for retrieval in the current MIB view, the receiving entity must generate a GetResponse-PDU with the following modifications: set the error-status field to "noSuchName", set the error-index field to the position index of the problematic object in the variable-bindings list, and maintain all other fields identical to the received GetRequest-PDU. This response is then sent back to the requesting entity, terminating further processing of the GetRequest-PDU.

When a receiving protocol entity processes a GetRequest-PDU and none of the preceding error conditions apply (including noSuchName for non-existent or aggregate objects, tooBig for size limitations, or genErr for other retrieval failures), it must send a successful GetResponse-PDU back to the originator. This response must contain, for each object specified in the variable-bindings field of the original request, both the object name and its successfully retrieved current value. The response must set error-status to noError, error-index to zero, and maintain the same request-id as the received GetRequest-PDU, thereby completing a successful SNMP get operation.

When a receiving SNMP protocol entity successfully processes a GetRequest-PDU without encountering any errors specified in rules (1) through (4) - meaning all requested objects exist in the MIB view, are not aggregate types, fit within size limitations, and can be successfully retrieved - the entity must generate a GetResponse-PDU with the error-status field set to noError and the error-index field set to zero. This indicates successful completion of the get operation, and the response must also include the actual values of all requested objects in the variable-bindings field while maintaining the same request-id as the original request.

When generating a GetResponse-PDU in response to a received GetRequest-PDU, regardless of whether the response indicates success (noError) or any error condition (noSuchName, tooBig, genErr), the request-id field in the GetResponse-PDU must exactly match the request-id value from the corresponding GetRequest-PDU. This ensures proper request-response correlation between the SNMP manager and agent, allowing the originating application to correctly associate responses with their original requests. This rule applies during the response generation phase after processing all applicable error conditions and applies to all GetResponse-PDU transmissions resulting from GetRequest-PDU processing.

The GetNextRequest-PDU is specifically designed for traversing conceptual tables within the MIB by leveraging lexicographical ordering of object instances. This rule applies when an SNMP management station needs to sequentially retrieve all entries from a tabular data structure (such as a routing table) without prior knowledge of the exact instance identifiers. The expected outcome is that each GetNextRequest returns the next lexicographically ordered variable bindings, enabling the management station to iterate through all table entries until it receives bindings outside the target table, signaling completion. Prerequisites include: (1) the agent must support the GetNext operation, (2) the requested object types must exist in the MIB with valid instances, and (3) the management station must initiate the sequence with base object identifiers (without instance suffixes) to begin traversal from the first available entry.

This rule applies during the processing of a GetRequest-PDU when all previous error conditions have been evaluated and do not apply. Specifically, it governs the scenario where the receiving SNMP entity encounters an object in the variable-bindings field that cannot be retrieved due to reasons not covered by previous error rules (such as noSuchName, aggregate type restrictions, or message size limitations). This serves as a catch-all error condition for retrieval failures.

When this condition occurs, the receiving entity must construct a GetResponse-PDU that mirrors the original GetRequest-PDU in form, but with the error-status field set to "genErr" (general error) and the error-index field set to the position index of the problematic object within the variable-bindings list. This ensures the requesting entity can identify which specific object caused the retrieval failure while maintaining protocol consistency with the request-response mechanism.

The rule applies only after verifying that the object exists in the MIB view, is not an aggregate type, and that response size limitations are not exceeded. It represents the final error-handling step before successful value retrieval in the GetRequest processing sequence.

During SNMP table traversal operations, when a management station needs to sequentially retrieve all entries from a conceptual table in the MIB (such as the IP routing table), it initiates the process by sending a GetNextRequest-PDU containing the base OBJECT IDENTIFIER values of the table columns as variable names. This rule applies specifically to the initial request in the traversal sequence, where the management station sends GetNextRequest(ipRouteDest, ipRouteNextHop, ipRouteMetric1) without instance identifiers. The expected outcome is that the SNMP agent responds with the lexicographically next available variable bindings, which correspond to the first complete row of the table. Prerequisites include: the management station must have knowledge of the table's columnar object identifiers, the SNMP agent must support the relevant MIB objects, and the table must contain at least one entry. This mechanism enables systematic retrieval of tabular data by leveraging the lexicographical ordering of MIB objects.
res_fields: first complete row of the table

rule: During SNMP table traversal using GetNextRequest-PDU, after receiving a GetResponse containing the first table entry (e.g., ipRouteDest.9.1.2.3, ipRouteNextHop.9.1.2.3, ipRouteMetric1.9.1.2.3), the management station must continue the traversal by issuing a subsequent GetNextRequest using the exact instance identifiers returned in the previous response. This rule applies specifically when sequentially retrieving all entries from a conceptual MIB table, where each GetNextRequest uses the object instances from the immediately preceding successful GetResponse to advance to the next lexicographically ordered table entry. The expected outcome is that the SNMP agent will return the next available table entries in lexicographical order, allowing complete table traversal. Prerequisites include: successful initial GetNextRequest, valid MIB object definitions, and the agent maintaining consistent lexicographical ordering of object instances throughout the session.

During SNMP table traversal using GetNextRequest-PDU, when a management station receives a valid GetResponse containing table entry data (e.g., ipRouteDest.10.0.0.51, ipRouteNextHop.10.0.0.51, ipRouteMetric1.10.0.0.51), it must continue the traversal by issuing a subsequent GetNextRequest using the exact object instance identifiers received in the previous response. This rule applies specifically when sequentially retrieving all entries from a conceptual MIB table, where each GetNextRequest uses the lexicographically next object instances from the prior response to navigate through the table. The expected outcome is that the agent will return the next valid table entries in lexicographical order, allowing the management station to progressively retrieve the entire table contents. Prerequisites include: (1) successful initial GetNextRequest that returned valid table entries, (2) the management station's intent to continue table traversal, and (3) the agent maintaining consistent lexicographical ordering of MIB objects throughout the session.

When processing a GetNextRequest-PDU for table traversal, if the requested variable names correspond to the lexicographically first entries in a conceptual table, the SNMP agent must respond with a GetResponse-PDU containing the actual values of the next available table entries. Specifically, when the management station sends GetNextRequest(ipRouteDest, ipRouteNextHop, ipRouteMetric1) to traverse the routing table, the agent must return the first routing table entry's values: GetResponse((ipRouteDest.9.1.2.3 = "9.1.2.3"), (ipRouteNextHop.9.1.2.3 = "99.0.0.3"), (ipRouteMetric1.9.1.2.3 = 3)). This rule applies during the initial phase of table traversal when the management station queries base object identifiers without instance specifiers, and requires that the agent has a valid routing table with at least one entry and that the requested objects exist in the MIB with proper lexicographical ordering.

When processing a GetNextRequest-PDU for table traversal, if the requested variable names correspond to the last entries in a conceptual table, the SNMP agent must return the lexicographically next available objects in the MIB beyond the table's scope. This behavior applies specifically when the agent receives a GetNextRequest containing object identifiers that have no subsequent entries within the target table. The expected outcome is a GetResponse-PDU containing the immediate lexicographical successors to the requested objects from the entire set of known MIB objects, which serves as an implicit end-of-table indicator to the management station. This rule requires that the agent maintains a complete lexicographical ordering of all accessible object instances and that the management station correctly interprets such responses as signaling the completion of table traversal.

During SNMP table traversal using GetNextRequest-PDU, when a management station sends a GetNextRequest containing the lexicographically last entry in a table (e.g., GetNextRequest (ipRouteDest.10.0.0.51, ipRouteNextHop.10.0.0.51, ipRouteMetric1.10.0.0.51)), the SNMP agent must respond with a GetResponse-PDU containing the lexicographically next available objects in the MIB. This response signals the end of table traversal, as the returned objects are no longer part of the original table. The agent returns GetResponse ((ipRouteDest.10.0.0.99 = "10.0.0.99"), (ipRouteNextHop.10.0.0.99 = "89.1.1.42"), (ipRouteMetric1.10.0.0.99 = 5)) when these represent the lexicographically next objects after the requested variables, indicating no further table entries exist.

During SNMP table traversal using GetNextRequest-PDU, when a management station requests the next lexicographical entries after the last actual table entry (e.g., GetNextRequest with ipRouteDest.10.0.0.99, ipRouteNextHop.10.0.0.99, ipRouteMetric1.10.0.0.99), the SNMP agent must respond with the lexicographically next available objects in the MIB. This response serves as an end-of-table indicator, signaling that no further routing table entries exist. The agent returns GetResponse containing the actual values of these next objects (e.g., ((ipRouteDest.10.0.0.99 = "10.0.0.99"), (ipRouteNextHop.10.0.0.99 = "89.1.1.42"), (ipRouteMetric1.10.0.0.99 = 5))), which may represent the same objects with different instance identifiers or different objects entirely, depending on the MIB structure. This behavior applies only when the requested variable names correspond to the last entries in the conceptual table being traversed.

In SNMPv1 protocol implementations, when encoding or decoding PDUs within SNMP messages, the PDU types GetRequest-PDU, GetNextRequest-PDU, GetResponse-PDU, and SetRequest-PDU must be identified using their respective implicit ASN.1 tags 0, 1, 2, and 3. This rule applies during message serialization/deserialization when the protocol data unit is being processed as part of the message's data field. The expected outcome is correct identification and handling of each PDU type according to its specified tag value, enabling proper protocol operation for network management operations. This tagging scheme is a prerequisite for interoperable SNMPv1 implementations and must be consistently applied whenever these PDU types are used in protocol exchanges.

During SNMP table traversal using GetNextRequest-PDU, when the management station receives a response containing the last entry in a conceptual table (as indicated by the lexicographically highest instance identifiers), it must issue a subsequent GetNextRequest using the instance identifiers from that last response. This rule applies specifically when the management station is sequentially retrieving all entries from a tabular MIB object, such as the ipRoutingTable. The expected outcome is that the SNMP agent will return objects that are lexicographically next in the MIB tree beyond the table's scope, signaling the end of table traversal. Prerequisites include: the management station must have already received responses for all previous table entries, the agent must support the GetNextRequest operation, and the table must be accessible via the specified object identifiers. In the example, after receiving the response for ipRouteDest.10.0.0.99, the management station continues with GetNextRequest (ipRouteDest.10.0.0.99, ipRouteNextHop.10.0.0.99, ipRouteMetric1.10.0.0.99) to complete the table traversal sequence.

This rule governs how SNMP community profiles determine permissible operations on MIB variables during protocol message processing. It applies when an SNMP agent receives an authenticated message and must determine which operations (get, set, trap) are allowed on specific variables within the community's MIB view. The expected outcome is that variables are made available only for operations consistent with both their MIB-defined access type and the community profile's access mode. Prerequisites include: (1) the message must be authenticated for the specified SNMP community, (2) a valid community profile must be defined pairing an SNMP access mode (READ-ONLY or READ-WRITE) with an MIB view, and (3) the variable must exist within that MIB view. For each variable in the MIB view: (1) if the MIB defines the variable with "Access: none," it is unavailable for any operation; (2) if the MIB defines the variable with "Access: read-write" or "write-only" and the profile's access mode is READ-WRITE, the variable is available for get, set, and trap operations; (3) otherwise, the variable is available only for get and trap operations.

In SNMPv1, the protocol data unit (PDU) carried within the message data field must be exactly one of five defined types: get-request, get-next-request, get-response, set-request, or trap. This choice applies when constructing or parsing SNMP messages after establishing the message header (version and community fields). Each PDU type serves a distinct protocol operation: get-request and get-next-request for retrieving management information, set-request for modifying managed objects, get-response for returning results, and trap for unsolicited notifications from agents. The selection must correspond to the intended operation, with get-request, get-next-request, get-response, and set-request using the standard PDU format (request-id, error-status, error-index, variable-bindings), while trap employs a specialized structure (enterprise, agent-addr, generic-trap, specific-trap, time-stamp, variable-bindings). Implementations must correctly encode/decode the chosen PDU type using the implicit tags (0-3 for request/response PDUs, with trap following its specific encoding rules) to ensure proper protocol interoperability.

The PDU structure governs the format of SNMPv1 protocol data units used in request-response operations (get-request, get-next-request, set-request, and get-response). This rule applies when constructing or parsing SNMP messages during management operations between SNMP managers and agents. The PDU must contain: a request-id integer to correlate requests with responses; an error-status integer indicating operation success (noError(0)) or specific failure conditions (tooBig(1), noSuchName(2), badValue(3), readOnly(4), genErr(5)); an error-index integer identifying the specific variable binding causing the error; and variable-bindings as a VarBindList containing the managed objects and their values. Prerequisites include proper message framing with version and community fields, and the PDU must be encapsulated within the appropriate SNMP message type with correct ASN.1 tagging (0-3 for request/response PDUs). The expected outcome is successful protocol operation where requests are properly formed and responses accurately indicate operation status and results.

The Trap-PDU governs asynchronous notification messages in SNMPv1, where an agent proactively informs a manager about significant events. This rule applies when an SNMP agent detects a predefined condition requiring immediate attention, such as device reboots, link state changes, or authentication failures. The expected outcome is a structured message containing: enterprise (object identifier specifying the trap source type), agent-addr (network address of the generating entity), generic-trap (integer code categorizing the event type with predefined values 0-6), specific-trap (additional detail code always present), time-stamp (TimeTicks since last system reinitialization), and variable-bindings (VarBindList carrying event-specific data). Prerequisites include successful agent initialization and proper encoding of all mandatory fields, with the PDU being transmitted only when community-based authentication is established and the detected event matches one of the specified generic-trap categories or enterprise-specific conditions.

Variable bindings form the core data exchange mechanism in SNMPv1 protocol operations, governing how management information is structured within PDUs. This rule applies during all SNMP message processing when encoding or decoding protocol data units, including get-request, get-next-request, get-response, set-request, and trap operations. Each VarBind must contain a name field of type ObjectName that identifies the managed object and a value field of type ObjectSyntax that carries the object's current value or the value to be set. Multiple VarBinds are organized into a VarBindList sequence to enable bulk operations. The expected outcome is proper encoding/decoding of management data according to ASN.1 BER rules, ensuring interoperability between SNMP managers and agents. Prerequisites include valid ObjectName references from the MIB and appropriate ObjectSyntax types matching the MIB definition for each object.
